summary:
The sickle cell mutation involves replacing an adenine base with a thymine base, specifically changing glutamic acid to valine in the beta-globin chain of hemoglobin. This leads to abnormal red blood cells that can become rigid and form into characteristic crescent or 'sickled' shapes under low oxygen conditions.
To refine further:

The sickle cell mutation results from substituting adenine for thymine at position six in the β-globin gene sequence, causing glutamate to be replaced by valine; this alteration impairs normal erythrocyte function due to altered protein structure affecting flexibility during hypoxia. To ensure clarity while maintaining brevity within two sentences focusing solely on critical aspects directly related to answering what constitutes the core issue—namely identifying how exactly the genetic defect manifests clinically—we should emphasize both its precise nature genetically AND physiologically impacting cellular behavior significantly enough to cause disease symptoms like vaso-occlusive crises associated with reduced deformability of affected RBCs upon deoxygenation stressors encountered throughout circulation dynamics typical among patients suffering from homozygous SS genotype presentations indicative thereof.

This leads to abnormal red blood cells that can become rigid and form into characteristic crescent or 'sickled' shapes under low oxygen conditions.
To refine further:

The sickle cell mutation results from substituting adenine for thymine at position six in the β-globin gene sequence, causing glutamate to be replaced by valine; this alteration impairs normal erythrocyte function due to altered protein structure affecting flexibility during hypoxia. To ensure clarity while maintaining brevity within two sentences focusing solely on critical aspects directly related to answering what constitutes the core issue—namely identifying how exactly the genetic defect manifests clinically—we should emphasize both its precise nature genetically AND physiologically impacting cellular behavior significantly enough to cause disease symptoms like vaso-occlusive crises associated with reduced deformability of affected RBCs upon deoxygenation stressors encountered throughout circulation dynamics typical among patients suffering from homozygous SS genotype presentations indicative thereof.
